assertiveness_why: Harry asks well-prepared, topic-varied questions (meetings, product prioritization, org structure) with real follow-ups, but never challenges or contests any of Holmes's answers.
question_style: Open-ended and structured around a checklist of founder-life topics (founding story, scaling creativity, meetings, product prioritization, fundraising), closing with a quickfire round. Harry references his own frustrations (meetings, small-business creativity) as framing but doesn't push back on Holmes's claims.
tone: Friendly, energetic, and personable ('automatic kinship' over both being university dropouts), light banter in the quickfire, heavy sponsor integration (AngelList/Angeloop, Wunder Capital).
depth: Covers moderately technical/strategic ground — org design (Alphabet-style cellular structure), agile release strategy (dark launches, beta opt-in/out), and fundraising timing/positioning versus early competitors like TweetDeck — but stays largely narrative rather than probing numbers.
harry_quotes: What do you do to optimize the meeting process both for productivity and for simple enjoyment?
How do you choose which features go through the funnel and which ones don't?
What did you do well and what would you like to improve on?
learnings: Holmes credits his restaurant-industry years for teaching him to identify his own core competencies and hire specialists to backfill the rest.
He runs an internal 'labs' division exempted from standard compliance/process overhead to preserve entrepreneurial risk-taking at scale.
He supports Google's Alphabet-style cellular splitting of large orgs, citing Dunbar's-number-style limits on effective relationship management (~130 people).
Hootsuite uses dark launches and opt-in/opt-out beta releases to gather product feedback from a broad user base without disrupting the whole customer base at once.
Hootsuite differentiated from early competitors (TweetDeck, CoTweet, Seesmic) by targeting business tools rather than a consumer social browser, likening its vision to 'Basecamp for social media.'
notable: Holmes discloses he built a side product (OriStand, a cardboard standing desk) inside Hootsuite to solve his own back injury, treating it as a mini case study in internal entrepreneurship.
